questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with a 4-day history of worsening sore throat, now unable to swallow saliva. He has a muffled voice and difficulty opening his mouth. Temperature is 38.4°C. On examination, there is marked tonsillar asymmetry with the left tonsil pushed medially and the uvula deviated to the right. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Trismus, muffled voice, and uvula deviation are classic for peritonsillar abscess; acute tonsillitis is usually bilateral without mechanical displacement, and epiglottitis presents with drooling and stridor.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with a 1-week sore throat was treated with amoxicillin for 3 days but still has trismus and a muffled voice. He is now afebrile. Examination shows asymmetric tonsils, the right tonsil medially displaced, and the uvula deviated to the left. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Persistent trismus and uvula deviation despite antibiotics indicate a drainable abscess; cellulitis would not cause such marked mechanical displacement.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old boy presents with severe sore throat, high fever, muffled voice, and drooling. He prefers to sit upright. Examination shows bilateral tonsillar swelling, more prominent on the right, with the uvula pushed to the left. He has difficulty opening his mouth. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Uvula deviation and trismus are specific to peritonsillar abscess; epiglottitis typically has acute stridor, retropharyngeal abscess causes neck stiffness and refusal to extend the neck.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with a 3-day history of right-sided sore throat and inability to fully open his mouth. Temperature is 37.9°C. Examination shows right peritonsillar swelling with medial displacement of the tonsil and uvula deviation to the left. There is no stridor. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Uvula deviation and trismus are pathognomonic for peritonsillar abscess; cellulitis may cause swelling but not such mechanical signs, and epiglottitis would present with stridor.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman with recurrent tonsillitis presents with 2 days of left throat pain, mild dysphagia, and fever 38.1°C. She has mild trismus and the uvula is slightly deviated to the right. Examination shows a left peritonsillar bulge. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Even mild trismus and uvula deviation indicate abscess formation; acute tonsillitis is bilateral without deviation, and peritonsillar cellulitis lacks these mechanical signs.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with severe sore throat, trismus, muffled 'hot potato' voice, and deviation of the uvula to the right. She has marked unilateral peritonsillar swelling. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Needle aspiration of the peritonsillar area
questions.explanation: Needle aspiration yields pus, confirming peritonsillar abscess and providing therapeutic drainage. In typical cases, it is the definitive diagnostic test. CT is reserved for atypical presentations or suspected deep neck involvement. Intraoral ultrasound is less readily available. Throat swab identifies pathogens but does not confirm abscess. Nasendoscopy assesses airway, not the abscess itself.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man has a 5-day history of sore throat with mild unilateral tonsillar swelling and odynophagia. He has no trismus, no uvular deviation, and is afebrile. There is clinical uncertainty between peritonsillar cellulitis and early abscess. What is the most appropriate investigation to differentiate between these?
questions.explanation: Intraoral ultrasound can distinguish cellulitis (no collection) from abscess (collection) and is non-invasive. Needle aspiration is reserved for when abscess is likely. CT is overinvestigation. Blood tests are non-specific and cannot exclude a small collection.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man presents with rapidly progressive sore throat, trismus, drooling, and inspiratory stridor. He has marked peritonsillar swelling. What is the single most appropriate investigation to assess airway patency?
questions.explanation: Flexible nasendoscopy allows direct visualisation of the upper airway to assess for compromise, and can be done rapidly at the bedside. CT is useful for deep neck infection but takes time. Lateral neck X-ray is less sensitive. Peak flow and ABG are not appropriate for focal airway obstruction.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with known peritonsillar abscess develops worsening neck pain, fever, and difficulty swallowing. There is tender cervical lymphadenopathy and neck swelling. What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm suspected deep neck space infection?
options: Needle aspiration of the peritonsillar area
questions.explanation: CT neck with contrast is the imaging modality of choice for suspected deep neck space infection, accurately delineating abscess extent and airway involvement. MRI is less readily available. Ultrasound is limited to superficial collections. Needle aspiration may miss deeper extension. Swab is not diagnostic of deep infection.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman who is 28 weeks pregnant presents with a 3-day history of unilateral sore throat, otalgia, and mild trismus. On examination, there is peritonsillar swelling without uvular deviation. She is afebrile. What is the safest initial investigation to confirm or exclude peritonsillar abscess?
options: Needle aspiration of the peritonsillar area
questions.explanation: Intraoral ultrasound is safe in pregnancy (no ionising radiation) and can reliably identify a collection, enabling targeted aspiration if needed. CT and X-ray involve radiation. Needle aspiration without imaging may be unnecessary if cellulitis. MRI with gadolinium is avoided in pregnancy. Ultrasound is the safest first-line test.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with a 4-day history of severe sore throat, trismus, and a muffled voice. On examination, the uvula is deviated to the right and there is swelling of the left soft palate. Needle aspiration of the swelling yields pus. The patient has no stridor and can swallow saliva. What is the most appropriate first-line definitive management?
options: Incision and drainage of the abscess
Warm saline gargles and oral analgesia
questions.explanation: Needle aspiration is the first-line definitive drainage procedure for peritonsillar abscess. Incision and drainage is an alternative, but more invasive. Antibiotics alone are insufficient because drainage is required. Emergency tonsillectomy is reserved for airway compromise. Conservative measures are not definitive.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with stridor, drooling, and inability to open her mouth due to a left-sided peritonsillar abscess. There is significant trismus and the airway appears threatened. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Incision and drainage under local anaesthetic
questions.explanation: In the presence of airway compromise (stridor, severe trismus), emergency tonsillectomy (quinsy tonsillectomy) provides definitive drainage and secures the airway. Needle aspiration or incision and drainage may be difficult due to trismus. Antibiotics alone are insufficient. Nasopharyngeal airway is not a definitive treatment.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 20 weeks gestation presents with a 3-day history of sore throat, dysphagia, and a peritonsillar abscess confirmed on examination. She has no airway compromise. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Incision and drainage under general anaesthetic
Needle aspiration of the abscess under local anaesthetic
questions.explanation: Needle aspiration is safe and effective in pregnancy. Incision and drainage under GA increases risk to the fetus. Elective tonsillectomy is not first-line and GA should be avoided if possible. Antibiotics alone are insufficient for drainage. Observation is not appropriate for a confirmed abscess.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with his third episode of quinsy in the past 18 months. Each previous episode has been treated with needle aspiration and a course of co-amoxiclav. The current abscess is managed with needle aspiration and the patient improves. What is the most appropriate definitive management to prevent recurrence?
options: No further action as recurrence is unlikely
Needle aspiration and antibiotic for each recurrence
Quinsy tonsillectomy (hot tonsillectomy) during this admission
questions.explanation: For recurrent quinsy, interval tonsillectomy (bilateral tonsillectomy after the acute episode resolves) is the definitive treatment to prevent further episodes. Hot tonsillectomy is not indicated unless airway compromise. Prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended. Expectant management is inappropriate given the recurrence frequency.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old patient with a peritonsillar abscess has known congenital long QT syndrome. Which of the following antibiotics is CONTRAINDICATED?
questions.explanation: Clarithromycin is a macrolide antibiotic that prolongs the QT interval and is contraindicated in patients with pre-existing long QT syndrome. Co-amoxiclav, metronidazole, clindamycin, and penicillin V are not associated with significant QT prolongation and are safe alternatives.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of severe sore throat, difficulty opening her mouth, and a muffled voice. On examination, she has trismus, a temperature of 38.5°C, and a swollen right peritonsillar area with the uvula deviated to the left. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform needle aspiration of the peritonsillar space
Prescribe oral paracetamol and advise gargling with warm saline
Arrange urgent CT neck with contrast
Admit for intravenous fluids and observation
questions.explanation: Needle aspiration is both diagnostic and therapeutic for peritonsillar abscess. Antibiotics are important but not the immediate next step before drainage. CT is reserved for atypical presentations or suspicion of deep neck space infection.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with quinsy diagnosed 24 hours ago was treated with needle aspiration and started on intravenous antibiotics. He remains febrile (38.2°C), has persistent severe pain, and still cannot fully open his mouth. Repeat examination shows no improvement in the peritonsillar swelling. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Request urgent CT neck with contrast
Repeat needle aspiration in the same session
Increase the dose of intravenous antibiotics
Discharge with oral antibiotics and arrange outpatient follow-up
Arrange incision and drainage under general anaesthesia
questions.explanation: Failure to improve after initial aspiration and 24 hours of antibiotics indicates inadequate drainage. Formal incision and drainage under GA is the definitive next step. Repeat needle aspiration may be attempted but is less likely to succeed. CT is not needed if the diagnosis is clear.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with a known peritonsillar abscess develops sudden onset of stridor, drooling, and inability to swallow her own saliva. She is sitting upright, distressed, with a muffled 'hot potato' voice. Oxygen saturations are 92% on air. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange urgent CT neck to assess airway
Call senior anaesthetist for urgent intubation
Perform needle aspiration of the peritonsillar space
questions.explanation: Airway compromise is an emergency; securing the airway takes precedence. Intubation may be difficult due to swelling; senior anaesthetic help is needed. Cricothyroidotomy is a rescue option if intubation fails. Needle aspiration may improve airway but is not the immediate first step.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man has recovered from his second episode of quinsy in 6 months, both treated with needle aspiration and antibiotics. He is now symptom-free, and examination of the oropharynx is normal. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Prescribe a 7-day course of oral antibiotics as prophylaxis
Perform a repeat needle aspiration of the tonsillar area
Schedule a follow-up appointment in 3 months
Arrange an MRI of the neck to look for underlying pathology
questions.explanation: Recurrent quinsy is an indication for tonsillectomy to prevent further episodes. Prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended. MRI is unnecessary without suspicion of malignancy. Further observation is not appropriate given the recurrence.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman who is 28 weeks pregnant presents with a 4-day history of right-sided sore throat, fever, and trismus. Examination reveals a bulging right peritonsillar area with uvular deviation. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform needle aspiration of the peritonsillar space with local anaesthetic
Prescribe oral paracetamol and advise saltwater gargles
Start intravenous co-amoxiclav and arrange ultrasound-guided aspiration
Arrange urgent CT neck with contrast, with abdominal shielding
Refer to ENT for incision and drainage under general anaesthesia
questions.explanation: Needle aspiration is safe and effective in pregnancy, and should be performed promptly. Antibiotics are adjunctive but not the first step. Ultrasound guidance is not standard for quinsy; palpation-guided aspiration is usual. CT is avoided in pregnancy unless essential. GA is reserved for failures.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old female presents with a unilateral sore throat, fever, and muffled voice. Examination reveals the uvula is deviated to the right. Which single finding most strongly suggests a peritonsillar abscess rather than acute tonsillitis?
questions.explanation: Trismus (difficulty opening the mouth) is a key discriminating sign, as inflammation of the pterygoid muscles in peritonsillar abscess causes spasm; it is not typically seen in uncomplicated tonsillitis. Uvular deviation can occur in severe tonsillitis without abscess, but trismus is more specific.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man with a 3-day history of sore throat now has difficulty breathing and cannot swallow his saliva. He is sitting forward and drooling. Which single finding is most concerning and warrants immediate intervention?
questions.explanation: Stridor indicates upper airway obstruction, a life-threatening complication of peritonsillar abscess that requires immediate airway assessment and intervention. Tachycardia and fever are non-specific signs of infection.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes is being treated for a peritonsillar abscess with antibiotics and drainage. Two days later, she returns with worsening neck pain and fever. On exam, there is a tender, warm mass in the left neck. What complication does this presentation most likely represent?
questions.explanation: Spread of infection from a peritonsillar abscess into the parapharyngeal space presents with neck pain, fever, and a tender neck mass. This is a deep neck space infection that can lead to sepsis or airway compromise. Retropharyngeal abscess typically causes difficulty in neck extension.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man on chemotherapy for lymphoma presents with severe sore throat and difficulty swallowing. Examination reveals bilateral peritonsillar swelling and trismus. Which single clinical feature raises the highest concern for a poor outcome?
questions.explanation: Bilateral peritonsillar abscesses are rare but more likely in immunocompromised patients and can cause rapid airway compromise. The immunosuppressed state also increases the risk of severe infection and systemic spread. Trismus and muffled voice are common in unilateral quinsy.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with a peritonsillar abscess underwent needle aspiration 24 hours ago and is on oral antibiotics. She returns with persistent fever and inability to open her mouth. What finding most strongly suggests inadequate drainage of the abscess?
questions.explanation: Persistent trismus indicates ongoing inflammation of the pterygoid muscles from a residual abscess cavity; this is the most specific sign that drainage was incomplete. Fever and raised inflammatory markers are non-specific and can persist even with adequate drainage due to surrounding cellulitis.
